document.title: Cyber Security Risk Management Report
standards: Internet of Things Security Test and Certification Criteria Guidebook
Standard for Technical and Administrative Measures to Protect Personal Information

sourceSummary.oneLine: Manufacturer cybersecurity risk management report for AspenView software, Revision 3 dated 2024.09.01.
sourceSummary.officialUse: Intended to satisfy regulatory requirements for medical image acquisition devices and document cybersecurity risk analysis, mitigation, residual risk acceptability, and post-production cybersecurity management for AspenView software.
keyPoints: AspenView is described as a software package for receiving, storing, distributing and displaying images and associated data in a clinical environment.
The product common name is Radiological Image Processing System, model name AspenView, device classification class II.
The report identifies cybersecurity hazards including unauthorized access, information forgery, unauthorized updates, lack of logs, and communication denial of service.
Risk controls include user authentication, access control, password rules, encryption, update authentication, logging, and security threat detection measures.
Overall residual risk acceptability is reported as acceptable for listed risk controls.
Post-market cybersecurity management includes monitoring NIST NVD, CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog, third-party software manufacturers, researchers, and advisories.

evidence.claim: The document is Revision No. 3 of a Cyber Security Risk Management Report for AspenView software.
evidence.evidenceText: Cyber Security Risk Management Report Model: AspenView software Revision No. 3

evidence.claim: The document revision date is 2024.09.01.
evidence.evidenceText: Revision Date. 2024.09.01

evidence.claim: Revision 03 was effective on 2024.09.01 and supplemented security architecture diagrams.
evidence.evidenceText: 03 2024.09.01 2024.09.01 Supplementing Security Architecture Diagrams
evidence.pageTarget: Cybersecurity Risk Management Report Revision History

evidence.claim: AspenView software is manufactured by Aspen Imaging Healthcare Inc.
evidence.evidenceText: AspenView Software manufactured by Aspen Imaging Healthcare Inc

evidence.claim: The report is intended to satisfy regulatory requirements for medical image acquisition devices developed, produced and sold by the company.
evidence.evidenceText: This risk management report is intended to first satisfy regulatory requirements for medical image acquisition devices developed, produced and sold by our company.

evidence.claim: AspenView is a class II Radiological Image Processing System.
evidence.evidenceText: Common name: Radiological Image Processing System - Model name: AspenView - Device classification: class II

evidence.claim: AspenView is intended for trained medical professionals and is not indicated for mammography.
evidence.evidenceText: AspenView is intended to be used by trained medical professionals including physicians, radiologists, nurses and medical technicians. This device is not indicated for use in mammography.

evidence.claim: AspenView can receive patient information and send x-ray images to remote destinations using DICOM 3.0.
evidence.evidenceText: AspenView includes the ability to receive patient information and send x ray images to remote destinations using the DICOM 3.0 protocol.

evidence.claim: AspenView expected service life is approximately 10 years based on the Windows OS update cycle.
evidence.evidenceText: the expected service life of our device has been determined to be approximately 10 years based on the window OS update cycle.

evidence.claim: The AspenView SBOM lists Microsoft Visual Studio 2022 and multiple third-party modules.
evidence.evidenceText: Software Bill of Materials for AspenView Module Name Version Microsoft Visual Studio 2022

evidence.claim: AspenView security architecture includes AES key and IV generation using .NET cryptography.
evidence.evidenceText: The AES key and IV values are generated when AspenView is first executed and are stored in the WorkList.db. The key and IV are generated using .NET's System.Security.Cryptography.Aes and System.Security.Cryptography.RandomNumberGenerator.

evidence.claim: AspenView encrypts specified DICOM tags using AES256.
evidence.evidenceText: This module encrypts DICOM tags. It encrypts the following tags using AES256

evidence.claim: AspenView automatically logs out the user after 10 minutes without mouse or keyboard events.
evidence.evidenceText: The user will be automatically logged out if no mouse or keyboard events occur for a certain period of time (10 minutes).

evidence.claim: The risk analysis identifies unauthorized access, information forgery, platform or hardware vulnerability, absence of system log, and communication service denial as cybersecurity hazards.
evidence.evidenceText: Identification of hazards No. Hazard ... Unauthorized access ... Information forgery ... Platform or hardware vulnerability ... Absence of system log ... Communication service denial, communication delay, etc.

evidence.claim: Risk evaluation by type of test counted 9 vulnerability scanning items, 1 white box test item, 3 network security test items, and 6 penetration test items.
evidence.evidenceText: Risk Evaluation by Type of Test Security Test Method Count Vulnerability Scanning 9 White Box Test 1 Network Security Test 3 Penetration Test 6

evidence.claim: The total risk evaluation score was 86 out of a total possible score of 95.
evidence.evidenceText: Total Possible Risk Evaluation Score is 95 (higher is better) ... Total 86

evidence.claim: AspenView risk controls include unique ID/password authentication and differentiated access authority.
evidence.evidenceText: User identification and authentication functions are implemented with ID/PW unique to each account. The authority for the information that can be accessed by each account is differentially granted.

evidence.claim: AspenView restricts multiple access to the same account.
evidence.evidenceText: AspenView restricts multiple access to the same account by checking the session information of the account.

evidence.claim: AspenView restricts account access after more than five incorrect password entries.
evidence.evidenceText: AspenView detects continuous login attempts using an incorrect PW, and restricts access to the account if the PW is incorrectly entered more than 5 times

evidence.claim: AspenView password rules require at least 12 alphanumeric characters and a special character.
evidence.evidenceText: Password rules have been reinforced to require at least 12 alpha-numeric characters and a special character when creating an account.

evidence.claim: AspenView records logs for access records, patient information inquiry, data creation, change, and deletion on a daily basis.
evidence.evidenceText: log information such as access records, patient information inquiry, data creation, change, and deletion are thoroughly recorded by AspenView on daily basis

evidence.claim: The overall residual risk acceptability assessment lists all risk controls as acceptable.
evidence.evidenceText: Evaluation of overall residual risk acceptability ... RM-RC-01-01 ... Acceptable ... RM-RC-13 ... Acceptable

evidence.claim: Risk management for AspenView software was reported as verified.
evidence.evidenceText: Risk management for AspenView software has been verified.

evidence.claim: Post-market cybersecurity monitoring sources include NIST NVD, CISA Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog, third-party software manufacturers, researchers, and external cybersecurity advisories.
evidence.evidenceText: Sources: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) National Vulnerability Database (NVD) ... Cybersecurity and Infrastructure Security Agency (CISA) Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog ... Third-party software manufacturers ... Independent security researchers and external cybersecurity advisories

evidence.claim: Post-market vulnerability monitoring frequency includes monthly NIST NVD and CISA checks, weekly third-party software advisory reviews, and quarterly security audits and assessments.
evidence.evidenceText: Monthly checks for updates from NIST NVD and CISA ... Weekly reviews of third-party software updates and advisories ... Quarterly comprehensive security audits and assessments

evidence.claim: Patch release timelines are one week for critical vulnerabilities, two weeks for high-risk vulnerabilities, one month for medium-risk vulnerabilities, and three months for low-risk vulnerabilities.
evidence.evidenceText: Critical Vulnerabilities: Patches developed and released within 1 week. High-Risk Vulnerabilities: Patches developed and released within 2 weeks. Medium-Risk Vulnerabilities: Patches developed and released within 1 month. Low-Risk Vulnerabilities: Patches developed and released within 3 months.

evidence.claim: The cybersecurity safety level for the device is marked Moderate.
evidence.evidenceText: Cyber security safety level: □Major ■Moderate □Minor

evidence.claim: The communication technology used is TCP/IP socket-based network interface and LAN communication.
evidence.evidenceText: Communication technology used: TCP/IP Socket-based Network Interface, LAN communication

evidence.claim: The checklist marks access control and authentication, unauthorized user recognition, unauthorized user blocking, password strengthening, password exposure prevention, system logging, integrity verification, and cybersecurity countermeasure information as applicable.
evidence.evidenceText: 1.1 Access control and authentication ... apply ... 1.3 Recognizing Users ... apply ... 1.4 Limiting the access of unauthorized users ... apply ... 1.9 Strengthening rules on creating passwords ... apply ... 1.11 Prohibiting password exposure ... apply ... 2.1 Recording system log for data audit ... apply ... 2.2 Verifying integrity ... apply ... 2.3 Providing information on countermeasures ... apply

reviewFlags.reason: Authority is ambiguous: the document is manufacturer-authored and references US FDA Recall and Food and Drug Administration, but the text does not show that FDA issued or approved the document.

Conflicting evidence on network/transmission functions: product description says AspenView can receive patient information and send x-ray images to remote destinations using DICOM 3.0, while checklist entries state no transmission function, no external connection function, and no connection function.

Conflicting evidence on session handling: security module and mitigation text describe automatic logout/session termination, while checklist item 1.8 states auto session close is not applicable because there is no automatic session termination.

Conflicting evidence on update controls: mitigation sections describe administrator-limited updates, password-protected installation, and digital signatures, while checklist items 1.12 through 1.14 state no update function.
OCR/text quality issues are present, including broken words, mixed Korean/English translation artifacts, and apparent typos such as 'deodorizes' and 'treat' for 'threat'.
